query: What is the Buddhist concept of nirvana?

This includes understanding the existence of sorrow, its cause, its cessation, and the path leading to this cessation. Nirvana is not simply about annihilation; rather, it is the ["extinction of all desires"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_173), *The Dhammapada*, leading to a profound peace and liberation.

query: How do different religions view the afterlife?
response:
Different religious traditions offer diverse perspectives on the afterlife. In Islam, the Qur'an emphasizes that ["every soul shall taste of death"](https://oceanlibrary.com/quran_rodwell-tr?paraId=para_292), and those who believe and do good will be rewarded with eternal gardens. Christianity, through the words of Jesus, promises that ["he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_425), highlighting the resurrection and eternal life. Sikh teachings reflect on the soul's journey, noting that it ["wanders through countless incarnations"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20186) until it finds peace.

Buddhism views the afterlife as a cycle of rebirths, with Nirvana being the ultimate liberation, described as ["the highest happiness"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17245). Hinduism, as expressed in the Bhagavad Gita, teaches that the spirit is ["birthless and deathless"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17365), emphasizing the eternal nature of the soul. Bahá'í writings liken the body to a cage and the spirit to a bird, suggesting that upon death, the spirit finds ["a paradise of delights"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16714).

In Taoism, life and death are seen as part of a continuous cycle, with wisdom lying in recognizing the ["immutable continuity"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18778) of these states. Judaism teaches that the spirit returns to God, as noted in Ecclesiastes: ["the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit returns to God"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18195). Each tradition offers a unique lens through which to view the mysteries of life beyond death.

Hinduism views prayer as a means to worship the divine, with the Vishnu Purana highlighting that ["he who recites prayers, prays to him"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17381). Christianity emphasizes the communal aspect, as Jesus taught that ["where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-matthew?paraId=para_482). Lastly, in Buddhism, taking refuge in the Buddha, Dhamma, and Sangha is a form of prayer that leads to well-being, as seen in the Therigatha: ["I go to the Buddha for refuge"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19673). Each tradition offers a unique lens through which prayer is both a personal and communal journey toward the divine.

Marriage is seen as a profound union across many religious traditions, each offering unique insights. In the Bahá'í Faith, Bahá’u’lláh describes marriage as ["a fortress for well-being and salvation"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kitab-i-aqdas_bahaullah), emphasizing its spiritual and communal importance. Christianity highlights the unity and sanctity of marriage, where a man and woman become ["one flesh"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-mark?paraId=para_316), as seen in the Gospel of Mark. Judaism also underscores the companionship in marriage, stating that ["it is not good that the man should be alone"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18124), suggesting that marriage completes one's life.

In Buddhism, marriage is a partnership where husband and wife should be ["in tune in virtue, in tune in generosity"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19031), fostering harmony and mutual support. The Qur'án advises living with spouses in ["kindness and equity"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16931), highlighting the ethical treatment within marriage. Sikh teachings often use the metaphor of the soul-bride and the divine Husband Lord, emphasizing a spiritual union and the importance of virtue, as in the Guru Granth Sahib: ["The happy soul-bride is always with her Husband Lord"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20190). Lastly, Hindu texts like the *Vikram and the Vampire* narrate traditional forms of marriage, reflecting cultural practices and the sanctity of the marital bond. Each tradition, while distinct, views marriage as a vital, sacred institution that nurtures both spiritual and social well-being.

query: Search for teachings about truthfulness and honesty
response:
Truthfulness and honesty are central themes across many religious traditions. In the *Tao Te Ching*, it is noted that ["sincere words are not fine; fine words are not sincere"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18783), suggesting a preference for authenticity over eloquence. The *Mahabharata* emphasizes that ["there is nothing higher than truth"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17366), highlighting its supreme value. In Judaism, the Talmud teaches that ["truth stands eternal and falsehood does not stand eternal"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18474), illustrating the enduring nature of truth.

In the Bahá'í Faith, truthfulness is described as ["the foundation of all the virtues"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_464), essential for spiritual progress. Christianity echoes this sentiment, with Jesus declaring, ["I am the way, the truth, and the life"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_532), linking truth directly to the divine path. Sikh teachings also emphasize truth, with the Guru Granth Sahib describing God as the ["Embodiment of Truth"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20184).

In Buddhism, the practice of Right Speech involves abstaining from falsehoods, as a disciple is to ["utter only the truth and are intent on the truth"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-tu?paraId=para_1484). Islam similarly upholds truth, with Ibn Arabi urging believers to ["speak the truth"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17097) in all matters. These teachings collectively underscore the universal importance of truthfulness as a pillar of ethical and spiritual life.

query: What do the texts say about the soul?
response:
The soul is a profound concept across the world's religious traditions, each offering a unique perspective. In Christianity, Jesus emphasizes the soul's eternal significance, asking, ["what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-mark?paraId=para_265), *The Gospel of Mark*. Sikh teachings in the *Guru Granth Sahib* describe the soul as intimately connected with the divine, where the soul-bride unites with the Lord through the ["Naam, the Name of the Infinite Lord"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20184). Hindu texts, such as the *Upanishads*, speak of the Atman, or Self, as the ["lord of the chariot"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17383), guiding the body and mind.

In Bahá'í thought, 'Abdu'l-Bahá explains that souls existed before coming to this world, but not in the form we understand on this planet, highlighting a pre-existence that is ["not as we know personality upon this planet"](https://siftersearch.com/document/11408), *Dwight Barstow Collection*. The Qur'án in Islam speaks of the soul's divine connection, where the Spirit descends upon those chosen by God, emphasizing that ["He causes the Spirit to descend upon any Servant He willeth"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16933), *Sermon of Recognition with Luminousness*. In Judaism, the soul is seen as a multi-layered entity, with the nephesh, ruach, and neshamah representing different aspects of spiritual life, as described in the *Sepher Ha-Zohar*: ["the nephesh and ruach are the two angles at the base of a triangle"](https://oceanlibrary.com/book-of-light_nurho-de-manhar?paraId=para_964).

Buddhism, however, challenges the notion of a permanent self, teaching that the self is not found in the aggregates of existence, as seen in the *Sutra Collection*: ["consciousness is not the self"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-efg?paraId=para_1222). Each tradition offers a distinct lens through which to understand the soul, reflecting diverse yet profound insights into human existence and spirituality.

query: What do religious texts say about unity?
response:
Religious texts across various traditions emphasize unity as a fundamental principle. In Judaism, the Psalms celebrate unity with the words ["how good and how pleasant it is for brethren to dwell together in unity"](https://oceanlibrary.com/book-of-psalms-tehilim_david?paraId=para_2503), highlighting the joy of communal harmony. The Bahá'í Faith sees unity as the central purpose of its teachings, with Bahá'u'lláh proclaiming that ["the world is but one country, and mankind its citizens"](https://oceanlibrary.com/god-passes-by_shoghi-effendi?paraId=para_508), emphasizing a global vision of oneness. Sikhism, through the Guru Granth Sahib, underscores the idea of ["One Universal Creator God"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20185), promoting a sense of unity under a single divine presence.

In Christianity, Jesus teaches the importance of loving God and others, encapsulating unity in the commandment to ["love the Lord thy God with all thy heart"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-mark?paraId=para_399), *The Gospel of Mark*. Hinduism, through the Upanishads, reflects on the unity of the soul and the divine, suggesting that ["all is the Brahman"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17383), indicating an intrinsic connection between all beings. Islam emphasizes the unity of God and the oneness of His creation, with the belief that ["Our Cause is but one"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15295), as expressed in the teachings of Imam ‘Alí.

The Taoist tradition, as articulated in the *Tao Te Ching*, speaks of the Tao as the source that ["produces and nourishes all things"](https://oceanlibrary.com/tao-te-ching_lao-tzu?paraId=para_53), fostering a natural unity among all elements of existence. Buddhism, through the concept of Dharmakaya, describes an ultimate principle where all things are ["reintegrated, and all remaining within its inscrutable Oneness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-l?paraId=para_411), pointing to a profound interconnectedness. Each tradition, in its unique way, underscores the importance of unity as a path to peace and harmony.

query: Find passages about service to others
response:
Service to others is a profound theme across many religious traditions. In Christianity, Jesus emphasizes that acts of kindness to others are akin to serving Him, saying, ["Inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these my brethren, ye have done it unto me"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-matthew?paraId=para_711), *Gospel of Matthew*. Similarly, the Jewish tradition commands, ["open your hand to the poor and needy"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18667), *Deuteronomy*, highlighting the importance of generosity. The Tao Te Ching teaches that the sage ["does not accumulate for himself"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18783) but finds fulfillment in giving to others, *Tao Te Ching*.

In Buddhism, the Metta Sutta encourages radiating kindness ["over the all-encompassing world"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17271), fostering a boundless heart. Sikh teachings from the Guru Granth Sahib reflect on service through actions like feeding and nurturing others, emphasizing companionship and humility. Bahá'í writings assert that ["service to humanity is service to God"](https://oceanlibrary.com/promulgation-of-universal-peace_abdul-baha?paraId=para_43), *The Promulgation of Universal Peace*, elevating everyday work to an act of worship.

Islamic teachings, as expressed by Al-Ghazzali, liken the Muslim community to a body where ["if a limb of the body suffers pain, the whole body responds"](https://oceanlibrary.com/revival-of-religious-learnings-vol-2_imam-ghazzali?paraId=para_421), underscoring mutual care and empathy. In Hinduism, the Upanishads speak of seeing ["the same Self in all beings"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17383), fostering a sense of unity and service to others as a path to liberation. Each tradition, in its unique way, elevates service as a fundamental expression of spiritual life.

Buddhism speaks of liberation through non-attachment, where one ["clings to nothing"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutta-nipata_buddha?paraId=para_738). Lastly, in Islam, Rumi reflects on the ephemeral nature of the world, suggesting that true existence is found in God, as ["everything else is annihilated"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17105). query: Is there free will in religious thought?
response:
Religious thought presents a rich tapestry of perspectives on free will. In Sikhism, the concept of Hukam, or divine command, suggests that while all actions are performed ["by the Hukam of His Command"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20190), individuals are still called to recognize and align with this divine order. Judaism emphasizes choice, as seen in Deuteronomy where it is stated, ["I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse; therefore choose life"](https://oceanlibrary.com/jps-book-of-deuteronomy?paraId=para_895), highlighting human agency in moral decisions.

Christianity, through Paul's letters, suggests that salvation is ["the gift of God"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-epistle-to-the-ephesians?paraId=para_15), implying a divine grace that transcends human effort. Buddhism, on the other hand, teaches that understanding the nature of phenomena as ["not self"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17268) leads to liberation, focusing on the relinquishment of attachment rather than the exercise of will. In Islam, the Qur'an acknowledges that God guides whom He wills, yet it also emphasizes human responsibility in choosing the right path.

Hinduism, through the Bhagavad Gita, speaks of the importance of action without attachment, suggesting that ["application to action is superior to abandonment"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17365). Bahá'í teachings affirm human nobility and potential, as Bahá'u'lláh writes, ["Noble have I created thee, yet thou hast abased thyself"](https://oceanlibrary.com/hidden-words-of-bahaullah?paraId=para_74), emphasizing the capacity for self-improvement. Lastly, the Tao Te Ching advises acting without ulterior motives, suggesting that true harmony comes when one does not strive, as ["the sage does not act (so), and therefore does no harm"](https://oceanlibrary.com/tao-te-ching_lao-tzu?paraId=para_317). Each tradition offers a unique lens on the interplay between divine will and human agency.

The Taoist view, as found in the *Tao Te Ching*, suggests that life is about returning to one's root, achieving tranquility, and understanding the ["eternal"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18799) nature of existence. Buddhism, through the *Dhammapada*, speaks to the cessation of suffering through love, noting that ["hatred ceases by love"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_8). Each tradition offers a path to understanding life's purpose through spiritual growth and ethical living.

query: Can science and religion coexist?
response: The coexistence of science and religion finds support across various religious traditions. In the Bahá'í Faith, ‘Abdu’l-Bahá emphasizes that ["true science and true religion must always be in harmony"](https://oceanlibrary.com/promulgation-of-universal-peace_abdul-baha?paraId=para_565), suggesting that any conflict arises from misunderstanding rather than truth. Islam, through the Qur'an, acknowledges the divine creation of the universe, stating that ["God hath created you whatsoever is on earth"](https://oceanlibrary.com/philosophy-and-theology_ibn-rushd), which aligns with the pursuit of scientific understanding as a way to appreciate God's work. Judaism, as explained by Maimonides, holds that the universe was created with specific properties by God, and these remain unchanged except by divine intervention, indicating a respect for the natural order that science seeks to understand. Christianity, through Paul's writings, asserts that ["the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-epistle-to-the-romans?paraId=para_16), suggesting that the natural world reveals divine truths. Buddhism, through the Kalama Sutta, encourages a critical and experiential approach to understanding, advocating for teachings that can be ["verified and result in the genuine quenching of suffering"](https://oceanlibrary.com/sutra-collection-hijk?paraId=para_1108). These perspectives collectively affirm that science and religion can coexist, each enriching the understanding of the other.

query: What happens after death?
response:
The question of what happens after death is addressed uniquely across various religious traditions. In Islam, the Qur'án describes a return to God, where ["judgment is His"](https://oceanlibrary.com/quran_rodwell-tr?paraId=para_519) and the righteous are promised ["Gardens underneath which rivers flow"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_1351). Christianity speaks of resurrection and judgment, where those who have done good will experience the ["resurrection of life"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_171), while others face damnation. In Sikhism, the cycle of reincarnation is emphasized, with liberation found through the True Guru, as souls wander through ["countless incarnations"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20183).

Buddhism teaches that one's actions determine their rebirth, with evil deeds leading to suffering in hell, as noted in the Dhammapada: ["men with evil deeds in the next world"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17245). Hinduism, through the Bhagavad Gita, assures that the soul is eternal, experiencing a cycle of birth and rebirth until it achieves liberation, as it is ["birthless and deathless and changeless"](https://siftersearch.com/document/17365). Judaism reflects on the soul's return to God, as expressed in Ecclesiastes: ["the spirit returns unto God who gave it"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18195). Taoism, represented by Chuang Tzu, questions the distinction between life and death, pondering whether death may not be life after all: ["death may not after all be life"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18768). Finally, Bahá'í teachings describe a spiritual journey after death, where the soul is sanctified and enters ["the all-highest Paradise"](https://siftersearch.com/document/7149).

These perspectives highlight a rich tapestry of beliefs about the afterlife, each offering a unique view on the soul's journey beyond this life.

query: How do religions explain evil?
response:
Religions offer diverse explanations for the presence of evil, often framing it as a necessary counterpart to good. In Sikhism, the concept of Maya illustrates how ["emotional attachment and love of duality well up"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20183), leading individuals away from divine remembrance, *Guru Granth Sahib - Anand Sahib*. The Bahá'í Faith suggests that evil is ["nothingness and whatever is in existence is good"](https://siftersearch.com/document/15205), viewing evil as the absence of good rather than a force in itself, *Star of the West*. Hinduism explores the doctrine of karma, where actions from past lives influence present circumstances, suggesting that ["our acts are the results of those of our past lives"](https://siftersearch.com/document/19876), *The Great Indian Epics*. 

Judaism introduces the concept of the yetzer hara, or evil inclination, which is seen as a test that can ultimately bring merit to the righteous, as ["the purpose of their creation was to bring merit to the righteous"](https://oceanlibrary.com/beginning-of-wisdom_rabbi-aryeh-lieb-lifkin), *The Beginning of Wisdom*. These interpretations highlight a shared expectation of transformative renewal, though each tradition frames it uniquely.

query: What is progressive revelation, and which texts in the library discuss it?
response: Progressive revelation is the idea that divine guidance is revealed progressively over time through different messengers, each building on the teachings of their predecessors. In the Bahá'í Faith, this concept is articulated as the ["One Religion"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bahaullah-and-the-new-era_john-esslemont?paraId=para_719) that grows and evolves, with each prophet representing a stage in its development — from the "Bud" of Moses to the "Flower" of Christ and the "Fruit" of Bahá’u’lláh, *Bahá’u’lláh and the New Era*. In Christianity, this is echoed in the idea of a ["new covenant"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-epistle-to-hebrews?paraId=para_109), which builds upon and fulfills the old, *Hebrews*. The Qur'án also speaks to this continuity, noting that ["messengers indeed have been denied before thee"](https://oceanlibrary.com/meaning-of-the-glorious-quran_muhammad?paraId=para_842), indicating a succession of divine guidance, *The Meaning of the Glorious Qur’án (Pickthall)*. Sikhism reflects a similar understanding, with Guru Nanak and his successors seen as manifestations of the same divine light, as expressed in the Guru Granth Sahib: ["the One Light has two forms"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20190), *Guru Granth Sahib - Sri Raag*. Hinduism, through the concept of *avatárs*, describes Vishṇu's descents to restore cosmic order, illustrating a cyclical view of divine intervention, *The Ramayana of Valmiki*. Each tradition contributes to a broader tapestry of understanding, where divine truth unfolds progressively across time and cultures.

query: True spirituality is really just about being a good person, isn't it?
response: Not quite — true spirituality encompasses more than just being a good person. In Christianity, Jesus teaches that the essence of faith goes beyond actions, urging believers to ["love your enemies"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-matthew?paraId=para_100) and to cultivate inner virtues like humility and mercy, as seen in the *Gospel of Matthew*. The Bahá'í writings emphasize that ["the essence of faith is fewness of words and abundance of deeds"](https://siftersearch.com/document/11432), suggesting that spirituality is about living one's beliefs through actions. In Judaism, the prophet Micah distills the essence of the law to ["do justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18367), *Makkot*. The Tao Te Ching highlights virtues like ["compassion, patience, and humility"](https://siftersearch.com/document/18776), which align with the Tao's path. Islam speaks of sincerity and devotion, framing spirituality as ["sincere devotion and being true in Faith"](https://siftersearch.com/document/16933), *Sermon of Recognition with Luminousness*. Buddhism teaches that purity and goodness are central, as ["not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one’s mind"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_208) are the teachings of the Awakened. Sikhism calls for conquering the mind to achieve spiritual victory, as seen in the teaching to ["conquer your own mind, and conquer the world"](https://siftersearch.com/document/20185), *Guru Granth Sahib*. Hinduism, through the Upanishads, emphasizes the importance of knowledge and understanding in spiritual practice. True spirituality, then, is a profound journey of inner transformation and alignment with divine principles across these traditions.
